findings.label: Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
findings.evidence: Deck has 19 slides (minimum 8)

findings.label: Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?
findings.evidence: Revenue/currency figures found

findings.label: Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've identified a real market problem and sized it well, but investors will want to know you've actually talked to the people who need to solve it—add a slide showing 3-4 specific customer conversations (even just names, titles, and one-line quotes like "Developer A said 'we lose 40% of projects to system failures'" or "Facility manager told us maintenance costs run $15K/year per building") or early traction like a pilot with a villa developer or letters of intent from 2-3 property management companies that validate the reliability pain point you're describing.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like a checklist rather than a timeline—add a specific regulatory deadline or enforcement date that creates urgency for developers right now. For example, if Estidama's next compliance checkpoint is Q3 2025 and villas built after that date must meet stricter solar requirements, lead with "Estidama enforcement tightens in Q3 2025, forcing 200+ planned villa projects to solve the reliability problem immediately" rather than just naming the regulation. This transforms "regulations exist" into "developers have 8 months to find a working solution."

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel); Solar hot water mandated by regulation; Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law)

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature list—you need to show *how* the AI actually works on a real problem. Add one concrete example: walk through what happens when an installer in, say, Dubai inputs a villa's roof dimensions and hot water demand into HelioHelp, and show the specific output (e.g., "system recommends 8 collectors, predicts 18,000 kWh annual savings, flags hard-water polymer requirement"). This transforms it from "AI-guided sizing" into proof that the engine actually solves the scaling problem you're claiming.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've built HelioHelp as a core differentiator, but investors will want to know what's under the hood — specifically, which cloud platform you're using for the AI engine (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure?), whether you're building the AI models from scratch or leveraging third-party LLMs, and if there are any open-source dependencies that could create licensing or availability risks. Add a single line to Slide 19 like "Built on [Cloud Provider] with proprietary model training on [data source]" so you're not caught off-guard when an investor asks how locked-in you are to your infrastructure choices.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You're operating in regulated markets (UAE, Abu Dhabi, GCC) with mandated solar installations and commercial deployments at hotels and utilities — add one bullet to Slide 4 or 5 stating your compliance framework, something like "ISO/EN certified design + local building code compliance for GCC flat-roof installations" to show investors you've already solved the regulatory hurdles that could delay scaling. This matters because utilities and developers won't move forward without it, and naming it signals you're not discovering compliance gaps mid-deployment.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides show financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a one-slide assumptions page that breaks down your 2026–2030 projections into three concrete drivers: (1) unit volume growth by customer segment (e.g., "50 installations in 2026 via Rixos + EtihadWE pilots, scaling to 500+ via ALDAR partnership by 2028"), (2) average selling price per installation and how it shifts as you move from pilots to developer-led deployments, and (3) cost of goods sold per unit (e.g., "hardware COGS $X at 2026 volumes, declining to $Y by 2028 as local manufacturing ramps"). This transforms your Bear/Base

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.evidence: $350K seed funding: 18-month cash runway

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see the full unit economics story—specifically CAC and LTV for your core customer segments (hotels vs. villas) so they can understand how quickly you recover customer acquisition costs. Add a simple table on Slide 14 showing: for hotels, what's your average deal size ($207K for Rixos?), estimated CAC (sales/marketing cost per hotel customer), and payback period; same for villas using your EtihadWE pilot ($6K per unit, but what's your blended CAC across your developer partnerships?). This transforms "38–46

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy between the $213K contracted amount and the $300K projected revenue.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 14, your 2026 hardware revenue shows $0.3M, but Slide 12 only lists $213K in contracted deals—that's a $87K gap that investors will immediately spot and question your forecasting credibility. Clarify whether the $0.3M includes pipeline deals beyond the two named customers (like the strategic discussions with Abu Dhabi Ports or ALDAR), and if so, add a footnote on Slide 14 breaking down: Contracted $213K + High-probability pipeline $87K = $300K total, with specific customer names or deal stages for each bucket.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You're showing strong pipeline and unit economics, but investors need to see exactly how you'll deploy this new capital to hit those 2026-2027 targets—add a dedicated slide breaking down the funding ask (e.g., "$500K for: $200K manufacturing scale-up in RAK, $150K sales/ops team hires, $100K pilot deployment support, $50K working capital") tied directly to your contracted $213K and the Masdar/ALDAR pipeline milestones. This transforms the ask from abstract to executable and shows you've thought through the path from funding to revenue.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
findings.evidence: EBITDA positive 2027

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to the Rixos and EtihadWE bullets that states the payment status explicitly — something like "Rixos (live project, revenue received Q3 2024)" or "EtihadWE (contract signed, $X paid to date)" — so investors immediately see these aren't pilots but actual paying customers generating real cash. Right now they're reading these as project names, not proof points.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Right now you're showing capacity and partnerships, but investors need to see actual revenue momentum—add a simple metric like "Current monthly revenue: $X, up from $Y three months ago" or "Q3 revenue: $180K vs Q2: $120K" so they can feel the trajectory you're building. Even if the numbers are modest, real growth data beats potential every time.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer in Rixos that's been running your system for over a year—that's gold, so show it. Add a retention metric to your traction slide: something like "Rixos contract renewed for full 570-unit deployment after 12-month pilot" or "100% customer retention; Rixos expanding from pilot to enterprise scale." This turns your case study from a one-off win into proof that customers stick around and expand, which is exactly what investors need to see for a hardware+services business.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project), EtihadWE

findings.label: Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Name 3-4 actual competitors in the GCC market instead of "Legacy Solar" and "Heat Pumps" — for example, "Sunrun's flat-roof systems," "Daikin's GCC heat pump line," and "local players like [specific company]" — so investors immediately understand who you're beating and can validate your claims against real products they might know.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Your competitive table shows why you win against existing solutions, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Carrier, or a well-funded solar company decides to build a flat-roof-optimized system—add a "Barriers to Entry" row that spells out what protects you (e.g., "proprietary polymer absorber formulation with 3-year development lead," "GCC-specific thermal modeling patents," or "existing relationships with 200+ installers"). This transforms your comparison from "we're better today" to "we're defensible tomorrow."

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing the connective tissue that makes investors believe you'll execute—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who fill gaps and bring credibility in areas your core team doesn't own (like fundraising, scaling manufacturing, or navigating Middle East markets). For example, if you're based in Abu Dhabi through HUB71, naming a local business leader or someone with successful exits in thermal/cleantech as an advisor would immediately signal you've thought about execution risks and have smart people in your corner.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your team slide stating the vesting schedule for key founders and any equity cliffs—something like "Dr. Faisal Ghani: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" next to his bio. This shows investors you've thought through retention incentives and prevents awkward questions later when they dig into cap table details.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification and patents locked down, but investors in regulated markets will want to see explicit alignment with the specific building codes you mention—Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel—spelled out clearly on your compliance slide or in a dedicated section showing which standards your system meets and any pending approvals needed. Add a simple table showing "Standard / Regulation → SolarisKit Compliance Status" (e.g., "Estidama Pearl Rating → Approved for water heating systems" or "Dubai Municipality Code → Pending Q2 2026") so it's crystal clear you're not just compliant but actively tracking the regulatory roadmap that drives your market.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked down and a solid risk mitigation slide, but investors will want to know explicitly: are there any patent challenges, licensing disputes, or claims against your IP from competitors or prior inventors? Add a one-sentence statement to your IP section (Slide 8 or Appendix C) like "No pending litigation or IP disputes; patents validated through independent counsel and clearance searches across US, EU, and UK jurisdictions" — this removes doubt and shows you've done the diligence work, not just filed applications.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these match. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between these figures and the stated '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' is not explicitly detailed enough to confirm mathematical accuracy.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a simple math bridge showing how your market sizing connects to the $20M revenue opportunity — specifically: "5% UAE villa market share = [X villas] × [Y systems per villa] × [Z average project value] = $20M." Right now investors can't verify if that $20M figure flows logically from your $400M SAM, and it's an easy fix that builds confidence in your numbers. Use your actual Rixos data (38 systems, ~$207K) as the unit economics anchor to make the calculation concrete and credible.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, inconsistent spacing and formatting between duplicate slide content
findings.suggestion: Go through the deck systematically and fix every incomplete phrase and placeholder before sending to investors—start with Slide 6 where "Strong push for dec" cuts off (should read "Strong push for decarbonisation"), replace all [URL_REDACTED] instances with actual links or remove them entirely, and standardize spacing between the duplicate slide content (you have several slides appearing twice with inconsistent formatting). A quick 15-minute pass catching these will transform how polished the deck feels—investors notice these details because they signal whether you've actually reviewed what you're sending them.
